Carnegie-Whitney Grant from the American Library Association

availability.p: Periodicals and books published before 1 January 1923 are in the public domain. If re-publishing, quoting from, or reproducing images from this digital archive, we request that you cite it as follows: Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive, Center for Digital Humanities, University of South Carolina.

Periodicals and books published on or after 1 January 1923 may be protected by U.S. Copyright Law. All attempts to secure permission for republishing post-1923 material in this archive have been made. The names, logos, and identifying images, as well as the content of the articles, remain the property of the periodicals and/or of the authors and artists themselves. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder. Archival material published in this archive is used with permission of the institution or library that holds it. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder.
Responsibility for any use of the contents of this digital archive rests exclusively with the user.

bibl.title: An Additional Harrington Note on the "Lone Woman" of San Nicolas

title.hi: AN ADDITIONAL HARRINGTON NOTE ON THE "LONE WOMAN" OF SAN NICOLAS Edited with Commentary

div.p: IN A PREVIOUS ISSUE of THE MASTERKEY (Vol. 52, No. 1; January-March, 1978), I reported the exciting discovery of some ethnographic notes among John P. Harrington's papers which pertained to the "Lone Woman" of San Nicolas Island. These materials were found in Boxes 4 and 745 of his Chumash material culture and ritual notes, now housed in the Smithsonian Institution. Recently I came across some additional material on this interesting segment of California history in Box 747, which also pertains to Chumash ethnography. The edited notes are provided below. As with the first series of notes on this woman, the informant is Fernando Librado:

Captain Nidever and an Indian named Malquiares11. Malquiares, a Ventureño Chumash, told Fernando that he and Hilario Valenzuela visited the San Nicolas Island woman at the Nidever home in Santa Barbara (Hudson 1978: 24). We are now told that he had also taken part in her discovery. found a woman on San Nicolas Island rolling silver22. Unclear what is being described. The silver may refer to the Mexican silver dollar or peso (Hudson et al. 1978: 162, note 352). in her hands to make abalorio.33. Abalorio is shell bead money. Malquiares spoke to her at Captain Nidever's request. Malquiares told Fernando that when they encountered the woman, she sang the following song:44. The song is somewhat similar, yet distinct, from one given to Harrington by Fernando before (Hudson 1978: 25 and 28, note 14), whereby the person from whom Fernando had learned it was unknown. We are now told that this song, and perhaps the other version of it, can be attributed to Malquiares.

The woman sang these words. After hearing them Malquiares could sing them, and they later became deeply impressed on Fernando's memory. Once, when Fernando met Aravio,55. Aravio is conceivably Arabio Talawiyashwit ("Imitator"), a Santa Cruz Island Chumash man who was a member of the 'antap religious cult, resident of Santa Ines, and once performed the part of the coyote in a Chumash Devil Dance held at Mission Santa Barbara (Hudson et al. 1977: 28, 89-90). It is possible that Aravio knew something about the woman's language, given the known interaction of the 'antap cult with the Fernandeño and Gabrielino (Hudson & Blackburn n.d.). a very aged Indian from Santa Ines, Fernando recited these words to him, and at once Aravio translated the song for Fernando. The words meant: "I leave contented, because I see the day when I want to get out of this island."

When discovered the woman's only clothing consisted of a skirt made of feathers and an apron. Jacob [George] Nidever and his people were otter hunters. They made the woman a dress out of their trousers. It was a month that they stayed on the island before they found the woman. After they found her, she would not eat their food, but she did stay around their camp. Mr. L. A. M. Ortega66. L.A. M. Ortega, one of Harrington's informants between 1912 and 1915, was probably Luis Antonio Maria Ortega. Harrington's notes state that L. A. M. Ortega's father was Antonio Maria Ortega, whereas in another note he stated the father's name was Jose Manuel Ortega. Harrington went on to add that the Ortega family employed many Indians, and that they learned Chumash from contact with these people. Andres Ortega's mother, Harrington wrote, talked the Refugio dialect of Barbareño Chumash very well. says that he has heard that after the woman was removed to Santa Barbara, she was a great hand at eating tallow. The woman was taken to the house of Jacob Nidever's folks in Santa Barbara, an adobe situated close to where the Southern Pacific Freight Station now stands.77. Early maps of Santa Barbara indicate that the depot was located approximately where it is now: the corner of Chapala and Yanonali, not far from Burton Mound and the site of the adobe home of Captain George Nidever (Rogers 1929: 100). When the men first put on the dress they had prepared for her out of their trousers, she began to sing and dance. She sang this song:88. The song appears to be another version of the one reported elsewhere (Hudson 1978: 24); again we are not told who reported it to Fernando but, based upon the context in which the first song was learned, it would appear that it was Malquiares.

lg.l: kachnaualanalna'al (swinging in the dance when this line was sung)

div.p: The Indians made blankets, sometimes by using strips of grass woven together. They called this grass grama in Spanish; it has long roots. As late as — [they still made them?]; Fernando still — — of this stuff and the sandal from the San Nicolas woman.9The blanks are Harrington's. What is meant by Fernando in reference to the woman's sandals is unclear; the meaning may have been that the woman's sandals were made also from strips of grass woven together. A sandal, collected by Lorenzo Yates and said to have been found in a cave on Anacapa Island, is in the collections of the Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History. She made her skirt of trousers and put a man's shirt on herself. After that [she, they?] threw away her Indian clothes. The woman would go to get water at low tide and this was her drinking water.

list.item: 1. Malquiares, a Ventureño Chumash, told Fernando that he and Hilario Valenzuela visited the San Nicolas Island woman at the Nidever home in Santa Barbara (Hudson 1978: 24). We are now told that he had also taken part in her discovery.
2. Unclear what is being described. The silver may refer to the Mexican silver dollar or peso (Hudson et al. 1978: 162, note 352).

4. The song is somewhat similar, yet distinct, from one given to Harrington by Fernando before (Hudson 1978: 25 and 28, note 14), whereby the person from whom Fernando had learned it was unknown. We are now told that this song, and perhaps the other version of it, can be attributed to Malquiares.

5. Aravio is conceivably Arabio Talawiyashwit ("Imitator"), a Santa Cruz Island Chumash man who was a member of the 'antap religious cult, resident of Santa Ines, and once performed the part of the coyote in a Chumash Devil Dance held at Mission Santa Barbara (Hudson et al. 1977: 28, 89-90). It is possible that Aravio knew something about the woman's language, given the known interaction of the 'antap cult with the Fernandeño and Gabrielino (Hudson & Blackburn n.d.).

6. L.A. M. Ortega, one of Harrington's informants between 1912 and 1915, was probably Luis Antonio Maria Ortega. Harrington's notes state that L. A. M. Ortega's father was Antonio Maria Ortega, whereas in another note he stated the father's name was Jose Manuel Ortega. Harrington went on to add that the Ortega family employed many Indians, and that they learned Chumash from contact with these people. Andres Ortega's mother, Harrington wrote, talked the Refugio dialect of Barbareño Chumash very well.

7. Early maps of Santa Barbara indicate that the depot was located approximately where it is now: the corner of Chapala and Yanonali, not far from Burton Mound and the site of the adobe home of Captain George Nidever (Rogers 1929: 100).

8. The song appears to be another version of the one reported elsewhere (Hudson 1978: 24); again we are not told who reported it to Fernando but, based upon the context in which the first song was learned, it would appear that it was Malquiares.

9. The blanks are Harrington's. What is meant by Fernando in reference to the woman's sandals is unclear; the meaning may have been that the woman's sandals were made also from strips of grass woven together. A sandal, collected by Lorenzo Yates and said to have been found in a cave on Anacapa Island, is in the collections of the Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History.

p.bibl: Some J.P. Harrington Notes on the "Lone Woman" of San Nicolas. The Masterkey, Vol. 52, No. 1: 23-28. Southwest Museum. Los Angeles.
Social and Religious Integration in Native South-Central California: "The Northern Complex." Unpublished ms. Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History. Santa Barbara.
Tomol: Chumash Watercraft as described in the Ethnographic Notes of John P. Harrington. Anthropological Papers No.9. Ballena Press. Socorro.
The Eye of the Flute: Chumash Traditional History and Rituals as told by Fernando Librado Kitsepawit to John P. Harrington. Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History. Santa Barbara.
Prehistoric Man of the Santa Barbara Coast. Santa Barbara Museum of Natural History. Santa Barbara.
